title: Room temperature Purcell enhanced single erbium ions in silicon-carbide-on-insulator microring resonators
authors: Joshua Bader, Shin-ichiro Sato, Jeffrey C. McCallum, Ruixuan Wang, Shao Qi Lim, Alexey Lyasota, David Broadway, Brett C. Johnson, Sven Rogge, Qing Li, Stefania Castelletto
abstract: Spin-carrying single-photon emitters operating in the telecommunication C-band (1530-1565nm) are prime candidates for integrated spin-photon interfaces, offering seamless compatibility with existing fiber-optic infrastructure, an essential component for future quantum networks. In this context, erbium-dopants ($\text{Er}^{3+}$) are particularly compelling due to their exceptional emitter properties, including small spectral diffusion and long spin coherence times. However, their low C-band photon-emission rate and operation at cryogenic temperatures has limited the realization of this technology.   In this work, we demonstrate fully integrated single-photon emission from an ion implanted $\text{Er}^{3+}$-embedded into a 4H-silicon-carbide-on-insulator (4H-SiCOI) microring resonator operating at room temperature. By optimizing the mode overlap between the resonator and the $\text{Er}^{3+}$-defect, we achieved a $\sim$70$\times$ Purcell enhancement and recorded small spectral diffusion of $\sim$54 MHz. We further characterize the $\text{Er}^{3+}$ single photon emission via photon correlation g$^{(2)}$-histograms and investigate its performance under varying magnetic-field, demonstrating Zeeman splitting on single emitters.
summary: This paper demonstrates the first instance of single Er3+ ion emission in a microring resonator operating at room temperature. By using a silicon-carbide-on-insulator platform, the authors achieved a 70-fold Purcell enhancement and high-purity single-photon emission. This provides a promising path toward scalable, integrated quantum networks compatible with existing fiber-optic infrastructure.
main_problem: The difficulty in realizing efficient, scalable spin-photon interfaces in the telecommunication C-band due to the low photon-emission rate and the requirement for cryogenic temperatures in Erbium (Er3+) systems.
main_result: Demonstrated room-temperature single-photon emission from Er3+ ions in a 4H-SiCOI microring resonator with a ~70x Purcell enhancement and high single-photon purity.
method: The researchers used ion implantation of Er3+ into 4H-SiCOI, followed by fabrication of microring resonators, and characterized the system using photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectroscopy, lifetime measurements, and photon correlation (g2) analysis.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of 4H-silicon-carbide-on-insulator (4H-SiCOI) microring resonators containing implanted Er3+ ions, operating in the telecommunication C-band.
key_observables: Purcell enhancement factor, Q-factor, spectral diffusion, g2(0) (single-photon purity), Zeeman splitting (g-factor), and optical lifetime reduction.
important_parameters: Room temperature operation, wavelength ~1533 nm, loaded Q-factor of ~4.26 x 10^5, Purcell enhancement of ~69.8, and spectral diffusion of ~54.76 MHz.
main_assumptions: Assumed a branching ratio of 0.5 for calculating Purcell enhancement and lifetime reduction factors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the SEM image and spectral transmittance of the microring; Figure 2 details the experimental setup and PLE spectra; Figure S5-S10 include Lorentzian fits, Er-dose comparisons, and lifetime-dependent PLE data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of telecom-band spin-photon interfaces, describes the 4H-SiCOI platform and fabrication, presents the characterization of the resonator and Er3+ emitters, quantifies Purcell enhancement and single-photon purity, demonstrates Zeeman splitting, and concludes with a comparison to existing technologies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates significant Purcell enhancement and coherent control (Zeeman splitting) of a spin-photon interface at room temperature, which is a major milestone for scalable quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Characterization of the Er3+-implanted 4H–SiCOI microring resonator: a SEM image of a representative microring resonator (scale bar 100 µm). The inset shows a 45°-tilted view of the microring–waveguide coupling region (scale bar 4 µm); b Spectral transmittance of the implanted and annealed device, showing sharp cavity resonances across the 1530-1560 nm wavelength range; c Narrowest observed TE00 resonance exhibiting an full width half maximum (FWHM) of 458.85 ± 5.7 MHz at the identified center wavelength λ0 where measured data (scattered) is fitted with a Lorentzian distribution (solid line); d Overview of the identified FWHMs over exhibited wavelengths from all TE00 resonances shown...

Fig. 2b). By overlapping both obtained scans, we identified several enhanced Er3+ transitions as well as an overlap of the cavity resonances provided by the microring resonator. By comparing indi- vidual arrival-time intervals for a specific scanned section centered at 1541.172 nm from Fig. 2b, we can identify a dominant inhomogeneous peak with multiple arising Er3+-transition lines within the first 45 µs after excitation-extinction (as shown in Fig. 2c), which decay explicitly fast, indicating successful coupling to the resonator propagating mode [26, 39]. A small background signal was also identified when time-intervals of 45 µs to 150 µs are considered, which could arise from defects...

Fig. 2 Optical properties from single Er3+-defects within 4H-SiCOI: a Schematic measurement setup where modulated tunable excitation is coupled into the photonic device via a V-grooved fiber-array (VGA) and subsequently outcoupled and directed towards an SNSPD in a photoluminescence excitation configuration; b obtained PLE-spectrum from the 1.2×1016 Er/cm3 implanted ring resonator conducted via the grating coupler (blue) and in a classical confocal configuration (green), where the inset illustrates the utilized free-space PLE setup for ‘thin-film’ characterization indicating an excitation- application via a 10:90 beam splitter (B.S.); c Lifetime-dependent PLE-section centered at 1541.172 nm...

Fig. 3 Observed Purcell enhancement from the Er3+- defect: a observed lifetime-transients from the 1541.172 nm transition investigated within all three considered scenar- ios, where measured data-points (dotted) are fitted with a weighted single-exponential distribution (solid lines) b Overview of all obtained lifetimes, where vertical error-bar indicate the fitting uncertainty; c Overview of the calculated lifetime-reduction factors τthin-film/τon as well as deter- mined Purcell enhancement factors Fmeas. p,Offres which takes the off-resonance lifetime into account as well as the deter- mined transition-specific Fmeas. p,trans. factor.

Fig. 4 Photon emission autocorrelation measurement and magnetic field dependence from the implanted Er3+-defect within the microring: a maximum time bin difference from the observed 1541.172 nm (orange) and 1538.936 nm (blue) Er3+-transition excited with a few hundred nWs on-chip. The red dashed line indicates the critical threshold of 0.5 to identify single photon emission. Measured datapoints (scattered) are exponentially fitted (solid lines) b Observed magnetic- field dependence from the 1541.172 nm transition where a B-field is applied in 5 mT increments.
